digest.editor_note: Fellow Humans, the Middle East has decisively moved from geopolitical risk to geopolitical reality: U.S. and Israeli strikes have killed Iran's Supreme Leader, closed the Strait of Hormuz, and grounded aviation across the region. Oil markets are thin on reserves and long on exposure, and the ripple effects — from stranded travelers to energy-dependent state budgets — are only beginning to price in.

sentiment.label: Risk-Off
sentiment.context: U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, Khamenei's death, Strait of Hormuz closure, and a terrorism-linked mass shooting in Austin have combined to produce one of the sharpest geopolitical shock events in years, with oil markets stretched and air travel in chaos.

stories.headline: Modi Chairs Emergency Security Meeting as U.S.-Israel Strikes Kill Khamenei, Shut Hormuz
stories.body: Prime Minister Modi convened India's Cabinet Committee on Security after U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and triggered closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for Indian oil imports. With roughly nine million Indian nationals across the Gulf and West Asia, and airspace across the region effectively shut, New Delhi is managing both an energy supply crisis and a potential mass evacuation scenario. The meeting was attended by Defence, Home, External Affairs, and Finance ministers, alongside NSA Ajit Doval and RBI veteran Shaktikanta Das.

stories.headline: Oil Markets Enter Iran Conflict With Minimal Buffer; Brent Could Hit $80
stories.body: Oil prices rose more than 2% following the U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran, with Barclays flagging Brent crude could reach $80 a barrel on sustained tension. The market's core vulnerability is structural: spare capacity is thin, Iranian supply is now at risk, and the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20% of global oil trade passes — has been closed by Tehran. Any prolonged disruption compounds an already tight inventory picture.

stories.headline: Thousands Stranded as Middle East Airspace Closes; Dubai Airport Damaged
stories.body: Iran, Iraq, Israel, Syria, Kuwait, and the UAE closed their airspace following Saturday's strikes, leaving thousands of international travelers — including hundreds of Australians — stranded in Doha, Dubai, and Abu Dhabi. Dubai International Airport, which handles approximately 300,000 passengers daily and serves as a hub for Emirates, sustained damage, as did Abu Dhabi's Zayed International, used by Etihad. The Australian Travel Industry Association warned travelers against panic-canceling flights, noting they would forfeit consumer protections by doing so.

stories.headline: Austin Mass Shooting Suspect Linked to Pro-Iranian Sentiment; FBI Probes Terror Nexus
stories.body: A gunman identified as Ndiaga Diagne, a U.S. citizen of Senegalese origin, killed two people and wounded 14 in Austin's entertainment district before being shot dead by police. SITE Intelligence Group flagged social media posts expressing pro-Iranian regime sentiment and hatred of Israeli and American leadership dating to 2017. The FBI's Joint Terrorism Task Force has been activated, and Texas Governor Greg Abbott has ordered heightened security at energy facilities, ports, and the southern border.

stories.headline: California's Iran Exposure Laid Bare as Gas Hits $4.65; Newsom's Social Media Flexing Backfires
stories.body: Governor Newsom's attempt to use California's sub-$5 gas prices as a rebuke of Trump's Iran policy drew immediate blowback: California imports 63% of its crude from foreign countries despite holding 1.7 billion barrels of proven in-state reserves, making it among the most exposed U.S. states to a Hormuz supply disruption. The state's average regular unleaded hit $4.646 per gallon as of Sunday against a national average of $2.984, and Valero's recent closure of its Benicia refinery has further tightened local supply. A proposed per-mile driving tax adds to the political combustibility.

stories.headline: Amazon India Waives Seller Fees on All Products Under ₹1,000, Expanding Fee War
stories.body: Amazon India has extended its zero-commission policy to cover goods priced below ₹1,000, a tenfold expansion from the April 2025 threshold of ₹300, covering 12.5 crore products across apparel, electronics, and home goods. The company recorded a 50% surge in new seller sign-ups after the initial April policy, and the move directly escalates pressure on Meesho and Flipkart in the price-sensitive, small-seller segment that constitutes 70-80% of Amazon's 1.7 million-strong seller base. Amazon declined to quantify the revenue impact of the expanded fee waiver.

quote.text: Oil is the world's biggest industry, and it is also the world's most political commodity.
quote.attribution: Daniel Yergin, The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money & Power, 1991
